skill.name: agent-ui
skill.description: Guides users through adding a React and Next.js agent interface from ui.inference.sh, including proxy setup, streaming, approvals, widgets, and browser tools.
skill.summary: Add a ready-made agent UI to React and Next.js apps.
skill.author: inferen-sh

security_audit.summary: Static command-execution and weak-crypto findings are mostly false positives caused by Markdown code fences, CLI examples, and model reference text. The confirmed risk is moderate because the skill instructs users to fetch a remote shadcn component, install an SDK, and configure an API key for a third-party service.

medium_findings.title: Remote Component Installation Requires Review
medium_findings.description: The quick start tells users to run a package command that fetches a component definition from ui.inference.sh. This is a legitimate installation pattern, but it adds supply-chain risk because remote registry content can change outside this skill file.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command is explicit and points to an external component registry. The skill purpose makes the pattern plausible, so this is a moderate supply-chain concern rather than confirmed malicious behavior.

medium_findings.title: Third-Party API Key Configuration
medium_findings.description: The setup requires an INFERENCE_API_KEY environment variable and a proxy route. This is expected for the service, but users must protect the key and avoid exposing it to client-side code or logs.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The API key setup is directly documented and semantically tied to the proxy route. No evidence shows exfiltration, but improper integration could expose credentials.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fences Misclassified as Shell Backticks
low_findings.description: Most external command findings point to Markdown examples, TypeScript snippets, or tables rather than executable Ruby or shell backtick evaluation. They are documentation content and do not execute when the skill is loaded.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line review shows Markdown fenced blocks and inline prop names, not runtime code execution by the skill. The remaining actual CLI examples are handled separately as supply-chain risk.

low_findings.title: External Documentation and Image URLs
low_findings.description: The skill links to hosted documentation, an image asset, and component docs. These links create normal network dependencies for users reading the skill, but no credential transfer or hidden request is present in the file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The hardcoded URLs are visible documentation and asset references. I found no evidence that the skill sends secrets to these URLs.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm alerts map to descriptive text, a model reference, and Markdown table content. No hashing, encryption, signing, or password handling implementation is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines contain prose, headings, and model or table text. No cryptographic API or algorithm selection appears in SKILL.md.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote Registry Install Command
dangerous_patterns.description: The installation flow depends on a remote component manifest and npm packages. Users should inspect the downloaded component and package versions before adding them to production applications.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command and external URL are clearly present. The risk is supply-chain exposure, not proof of malicious code.

content.value_statement: Teams need agent interfaces that handle streaming, tools, approvals, and generated UI without rebuilding each layer. This skill shows how to add the ui.inference.sh Agent component to a React or Next.js app.
actual_capabilities: Shows how to install the Agent component from the ui.inference.sh shadcn registry.
Provides a Next.js proxy route pattern using the inferencesh SDK.
Documents the INFERENCE_API_KEY environment variable needed by the proxy.
Demonstrates Agent component configuration with proxyUrl and model settings.
Introduces client-side tools for form scanning and form filling workflows.
Links to related documentation for agents, approvals, widgets, and UX patterns.
limitations: The skill is documentation only and does not bundle the component source.
It assumes a React, Next.js, and shadcn-style project setup.
It depends on external inference.sh services, registry content, and documentation.
Users must review downloaded components and protect API keys before production use.

use_cases.title: Add a SaaS Copilot
use_cases.description: Use the Agent component to add a streaming assistant with tool approval states inside a product dashboard.

use_cases.title: Prototype Agent Workflows
use_cases.description: Set up a Next.js proxy and agent configuration quickly for testing model-driven UI workflows.

use_cases.title: Create Form Automation Interfaces
use_cases.description: Use the client-side tools example as a starting point for agents that inspect and fill browser forms.

prompt_templates.title: Install the Component
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup in an existing Next.js project
prompt_templates.prompt: Add the agent-ui skill to my Next.js app. Show the required install steps and list the files I should review.

prompt_templates.title: Configure the Proxy
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a safe plan for adding the inference proxy route and INFERENCE_API_KEY handling to my app.

prompt_templates.title: Customize the Agent
prompt_templates.prompt: Adapt the Agent component for a SaaS dashboard assistant with streaming responses and human approval for tool actions.

prompt_templates.title: Review Production Readiness
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit my planned agent-ui integration for API key exposure, remote dependency risk, tool permissions, and user approval flows.

output_examples.input: Add a chat agent to my Next.js dashboard.
output_examples.output: A setup checklist covering the component install, proxy route, environment variable, Agent configuration, and files to inspect.

output_examples.input: Help me design tool approvals for an internal assistant.
output_examples.output: A product-focused plan that separates pending, approved, rejected, progress, and result states for each tool action.

output_examples.input: Review whether my agent integration is safe for production.
output_examples.output: A security review covering remote component provenance, SDK versions, server-only API keys, upload settings, and approval boundaries.

best_practices: Inspect remote registry output before committing generated component files.
Keep INFERENCE_API_KEY server-side and out of browser bundles.
Require human approval for tools that change user data or call external systems.
anti_patterns: Do not paste API keys into client components or public configuration.
Do not allow unrestricted browser tools without clear approval states.
Do not upgrade remote component or SDK dependencies without reviewing the diff.

faq.question: What does this skill install?
faq.answer: It documents how to add the ui.inference.sh Agent component and supporting SDK setup to a React or Next.js app.

faq.question: Does it include backend logic?
faq.answer: It shows a Next.js proxy route pattern. The runtime behavior depends on the inferencesh SDK and external service.

faq.question: Is an API key required?
faq.answer: Yes. The setup uses INFERENCE_API_KEY for the proxy route and it should stay server-side.

faq.question: Can I use it with Claude, Codex, or Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill metadata marks it as compatible with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: What should I review before production use?
faq.answer: Review the remote component output, SDK version, proxy route, upload settings, tool permissions, and API key handling.

faq.question: Does the skill contain hidden executable code?
faq.answer: No evidence found in SKILL.md. The file contains documentation, examples, links, and installation commands.
